input.prompt: My ASP.NET Core (.NET 8) app running on a Windows Server has slow requests under load. I have admin access. Which trace should I collect to capture waits/blocks, and what PerfView arguments should I use?

data.content: My ASP.NET Core (.NET 8) app running on a Windows Server has slow requests under load. I have admin access. Which trace should I collect to capture waits/blocks, and what PerfView arguments should I use?
data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-04-05T00:44:58.840Z</current_datetime>

My ASP.NET Core (.NET 8) app running on a Windows Server has slow requests under load. I have admin access. Which trace should I collect to capture waits/blocks, and what PerfView arguments should I use?

input.initialPrompt: My ASP.NET Core (.NET 8) app running on a Windows Server has slow requests under load. I have admin access. Which trace should I collect to capture waits/blocks, and what PerfView arguments should I use?

data.content:
For waits/blocks under load, collect a **Thread Time (Wall Clock) trace** — it captures both on-CPU and off-CPU (blocked/waiting) time, unlike CPU-only traces.

**PerfView command:**

```cmd
PerfView.exe collect /ThreadTime /NoGui /AcceptEULA /MaxCollectSec:30 /CircularMB:512 /Zip:true
```

**Key flags explained:**

| Flag | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `/ThreadTime` | Enables context-switch and CPU-sample events so you see **waits, locks, I/O blocks, and async gaps** |
| `/CircularMB:512` | Ring buffer size — prevents disk exhaustion under load |
| `/MaxCollectSec:30` | Auto-stops after 30s; adjust to capture your slow window |
| `/Zip:true` | Compresses into a single `.etl.zip` for easy transfer |

**Analysis in PerfView:**

1. Open the `.etl.zip` → **Thread Time Stacks**
2. Filter to your process (`dotnet` or your app name)
3. Look at `BLOCKED_TIME` and `AWAIT_TIME` nodes — these reveal lock contention, synchronous-over-async (`Task.Result`/`.Wait()`), slow DB calls, etc.

**Tip:** If you suspect async issues specifically, also check the **"Any Stacks"** view with `Microsoft-Diagnostics-DiagnosticSource` events enabled for HTTP/DB correlation.
data.reasoningText: The user is asking about performance tracing for an ASP.NET Core (.NET 8) app on Windows Server under load, specifically about capturing waits/blocks with PerfView.
